source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
2. The CSCS and transnational law
Although the SCS has attained attention as a dystopian surveillance system
for private individuals beyond China, it is actually a wider concept that
encompasses the regulation of society and state in general. The SCS is not
(yet) a single uniﬁed and nation-wide system. The current reality of the
SCS is heterogenous and scattered across hundreds of regulations and thou-
sands of documents on the central and local level.27 There are various
23
   Mareike Ohlberg, Shazeda Ahmed and Bertram Lang, ‘Central Planning, Local Experiments: The
   complex Implementation of China’s Social Credit System’ (2017) Mercator Institute for China Studies,
   online:      https://merics.org/sites/default/ﬁles/2020-04/171212_China_Monitor_43_Social_Credit_
   System_Implementation.pdf 3 and 5.
24
   See Creemers (n 15) 2.
25
   Additionally, it needs to be mentioned that the analysis is based on translations of the main policy
   documents of the SCS. However, many articles of Chinese academics or scholars who are ﬂuent in
   Mandarin analyse the original sources as well as the current state of the SCS in detail. In the
   paper, I thus rely on the work of these scholars.
26
   The 2014 Planning Outline is furthermore regarded as ‘the most authoritative blueprint for sub-
   sequent SCS eﬀorts’, see Creemers (n 15) 4 and 12. In order to exclude the possibility of misidentifying
   what is the reality of the system and what is envisioned, I reached out to several experts who had
   analysed the system(s) and the current state of implementation.
27
   See European Union Chamber of Commerce in China (n 13) 3.
                                                            TRANSNATIONAL LEGAL THEORY                   299
